section_title: INTRODUCTION TO MARKET MAKER TRAPS AND FALSE BREAKOUTS
content:
Welcome to the eighth and final teaching for the second month of the ICT Mentorship, October. This
session deals specifically with Market Maker Traps, focusing on false breakouts. For the sake of saving
time, only one side of the marketplace will be discussed, with the understanding that the reverse applies to
the opposite condition.

section_title: FALSE BREAKOUT ABOVE CONSOLIDATION IN BEARISH MARKETS
content:
A false breakout above price consolidation generally manifests in primary bearish markets. If the market is
in a downtrend, trading lower, or expected to trade lower, a false breakout above consolidations is
reasonably expected. This typically occurs at some measure of equilibrium in price, where the market
moves into a trading range.

section_title: NEOPHYTE TRADER BEHAVIOR IN TRADING RANGES
content:
Neophyte traders, or breakout traders, will bracket the trading range with orders. This means they place:
- Buy stops to initiate a long position above old highs.
- Sell stops to initiate a short position below old lows.
They aim to capture a trend, placing orders on both sides because they lack a clear directional idea or are
long-term trend followers willing to take many small losses for a large trade.

section_title: MARKET MAKER ACTIONS IN BEARISH FALSE BREAKOUTS
content:
In a primarily bearish market, market makers will typically send price above the range to neutralize buy
stops. Their playbook is to run the buy stops above the marketplace, assuming a bearish underlying
condition.

section_title: FALSE BREAKOUT BELOW CONSOLIDATION IN BULLISH MARKETS
content:
Conversely, a false breakout below price consolidations generally manifests in primary bullish markets.
Similar to bearish conditions, this occurs at some measure of equilibrium in price, where the market moves
into a trading range.

section_title: NEOPHYTE TRADER BEHAVIOR IN BULLISH SCENARIOS
content:
Again, neophyte traders or breakout traders will bracket the trading range with orders, looking to focus on
buying on a breakout or selling short on a breakout. They simply want to react to whatever the market gives
them in terms of breaking out above or below.

section_title: MARKET MAKER ACTIONS IN BULLISH FALSE BREAKOUTS
content: In this bullish scenario, market makers will typically send price below the range to neutralize the sell stops.

section_title: BREAKOUT TRADER'S GRAPHICAL PERSPECTIVE
content:
Graphically, a breakout trader or neophyte trader's perspective on a market entering a trading range
involves:
- Buy stops above the old high or recent high for long breakout traders, and for anyone that would be short.
- Sell stops below the lows for long traders and for short breakout traders.
They aim to capture a move that would hopefully continue for a long period, getting short on weakness or
buying on strength when breaking above an old high, hoping to capture a long-term trend up.

section_title: BULLISH ENVIRONMENT FOCUS
content:
From a market maker's perspective, in a predominantly bullish environment, we look below the marketplace
where lows exist in the form of sell stops. Everything shown here will be for a buying environment; the
reverse applies when the market is bearish to save time.

section_title: SELL STOP ABSORPTION FOR LONG ORDERS
content:
When the market breaks below consolidation, those sell stops are used to pair long orders. It is smart
money that is buying those sell stops. Simultaneously, buy stops are resting above the highs.

section_title: PRICE EXPANSION TO TARGET BUY STOPS
content:
As the market moves away from those sell stops being run out below the consolidation, they are going to
expand price (think IPDA) up to the liquidity above the old high. Those buy stops are then used to pair long
selling, allowing smart money to scale out of long positions or take some profits.

section_title: SUBSEQUENT CONSOLIDATION AND SELL STOP HUNT
content:
Eventually, the market will go into another area of consolidation or another trading range. The market drops
below that trading range to take out the sell stops below consolidation.

section_title: INTERPRETING MARKET MAKER INTENT
content:
As soon as this happens, the internal thought process should be: 'Okay, they've already shown a
willingness to take the sell stops below (e.g., 108.55). Now they've rallied price above (e.g., 109 big figure),
we've retraced a little bit. If they are taking the market below this short-term consolidation, they're probably
going to run the price higher.' The objectives would be where buy stops are resting above that old high.

section_title: PURPOSE OF RUNNING SELL STOPS
content:
After taking the market below a consolidation, and with our understanding and expectation that the market
is bullish, they are taking the market below the consolidations to run sell stops out. The only reason they
want to do that is to absorb those sell stops to be counterparties for their longs.

section_title: SMART MONEY EXITS AND LIQUIDITY
content:
When smart money is long, how do they look to exit their positions and book a profit or hedge? They have
to get out in a place where there are willing participants to buy. Buyers are always above the highs, either in
the form of a buying breakout or buy stops on short positions. This is where the liquidity is, representing the
market paradigm shift.

section_title: CONFIRMING THE BUY MODEL AND PROFIT TAKING
content:
Price runs above that old high, and now we have two areas at which the market makers have booked long
positions, so now they're pricing in a buy model. As the market moves higher, they are going to liquidate
some of their positions to hedge, and also liquidate positions that those traders on the bank level are
actually speculating for profit. This is how they actually take their trades, put them on, and scale them off,
placing their entries and limit orders to get out with a profit.

section_title: CLEAN HIGHS
content:
What rests above that? Remember the specific things to look for in the first month: clean highs and clean
lows. There's an area right over here with clean highs above. From where we're showing right now, after
that 109.15 level has been hit, there really isn't much in the way of liquidity voids or pools of interest, except
for those stops right above those equal highs.

section_title: PRICE EXPANSION AND LIQUIDITY SEEKING
content:
As you can see, price expands. In the interbank price delivery algorithm, it's going to expand up and seek
liquidity. That's what we see here.

section_title: RECURRING BULLISH CONDITION AND CONSOLIDATION
content:
Then what happens? Price invariably will go back into a consolidation. What's the underlying condition of
the marketplace? It's bullish. Why? Because they keep taking price below and then rejecting that,
absorbing the sell stops and expanding price higher. Is there any reason to suspect that price could go
higher? Look at the price action.

section_title: ANTICIPATING THE NEXT SELL STOP HUNT
content:
If you don't see anything, we'll come to it as we move forward. But we have a consolidation in here. Our
reasonable expectation would be to see price seek the sell stops below the consolidation. Price does in fact
go below the consolidation.

section_title: LIQUIDITY ABOVE OLD HIGHS (CANDLE BODIES)
content:
What's above this old high? Liquidity. Do we always look at the old high or would we look for the wicks? We
can, but primarily all the volume is seen in the bodies of the candles. So the body of the candles has the
most volume, and the liquidity is going to rest above it.

section_title: ACCUMULATION AND REPRICING
content:
So if they took the stops out below the 108.95, they're going to look to run price higher in the form of
109.40 or higher. Initially we had that short-term consolidation, the buy stops resting above that, they could
scale off a position right there, and the next objective would be 109.40.

section_title: BUY STOP ACTIVATION AND ORDER STACKING
content:
As you can see, 109.40 is swept, moving just above the bodies of the candles. Note the way that the orders
are stacking higher each time. When they buy, they're buying a little bit higher each time, working their
orders in, and still offering opportunities to scale off and profit. So the hedgers can work this model, the
speculators at the bank level can work this model, and they're also providing liquidity for those individuals
that want sell stops to be activated if the market goes down to those positions, and those individuals that
want buy stops above the old highs, they're happy because their liquidity is being provided as well.
Remember that market efficiency paradigm.

section_title: THINKING LIKE A MARKET MAKER
content:
You have to think like a market maker. Now we have a larger trading range. I've kept that area where old
buying was done at 108.95, but now we've moved into a larger range. Nothing's changed here, the model is
still bullish.

section_title: LARGE RANGE SELL STOP HUNT AND LIQUIDITY TARGET
content:
So we see market action trade below that consolidation. It trades down into 108.85. With that movement
down into that level, where is the liquidity at? Above the old high, at 109.45 or so. That's what you have up
here, specifically buy stops. So we have willing buyers that are waiting up there for price once it gets there.

section_title: MARKET MAKER'S PROFIT CYCLE
content:
They're buying, smart money's buying at 108.90 or 108.85 in that area, and they know that there's going to
be willing participants to be buying it from them when they want to sell it for a profit at 109.45 to 109.50. So
109.45 to 109.50 is hit, so the buy stops are used to pair long exits.

section_title: CONSOLIDATIONS AND THE MARKET EFFICIENCY PARADIGM
content:
Now we look what we have here. We have a wonderful example of how using consolidations with the
expectation of viewing the market with that market efficiency paradigm I spoke about, where we think like a
liquidity provider, we can still speculate, we can still hedge, we can still make a profit using the same
model. It answers all of the problems as it relates to trading, but specifically it offers the answer to liquidity
providing. Buy stops are activated and they're actually given the ability to be effective, and sell stops are
the same way.

section_title: UNDERSTANDING MARKET MAKER'S ROLE: PROVIDING LIQUIDITY
content:
So what the market makers do, we can vilify them, we can say all those rascals, they did this to me, they
did that to me, okay, but what they're doing is their job. They're providing liquidity. The absence or lack of
understanding that traders have, they attribute that as 'those guys, they did it to me,' and they usually
misappropriate that to their broker. But what it is, is the market maker is providing liquidity.

section_title: LIQUIDITY AS THE PRIMARY DRIVER OF PRICE ACTION
content:
And if you understand how the market seeks liquidity, that's the number one driver in price action. The
market will always seek liquidity, and where is the most recent area of liquidity that's been untapped with
the least of resistance getting to it? And that will give you the directional bias when you're a trader. But
when you see markets go into consolidation and you have an underlying directional premise or you arrive
at one based on studying the market like this, you can quickly ascertain trade setups and signals will jump
off the chart at you by way of looking at it like this.

section_title: CONTROLLING PRICE
content:
Again, we have to look at price with that market efficiency paradigm. We do not look at it as 'give me a buy
and a sell.' Think like the market maker. If you had complete control of price, where would you drive price at
to allow other traders to get in and at least facilitate their trades, at least whether they're right or wrong?
That's not the problem here. Where's the buyers and where's the sellers at? And if we look at the market in
this scope, in this perspective, we'll know that we'll always have buyers above old highs and we'll always
have sellers below old lows. So if we know that that's where liquidity rests, all we have to do is discern
whether or not does the market want to seek the buyers or does it want to seek the sellers?

section_title: DISCERNMENT THROUGH CONSOLIDATION BEHAVIOR
content:
And the way you get that indication is by studying when they go into consolidation, what side of the market
they're reaching for, and then where is the market going after it happens. And here you can clearly see
every time there's a consolidation, the market seeks the liquidity below the marketplace, and once it
absorbs it, it quickly runs the other direction. Why is that happening? Because the market makers are
facilitating their long positions and building a buy model in the price or in this asset.

section_title: FALSE BREAKOUTS AS ACCUMULATION SIGNALS
content:
So we can expect to see every time the market goes below these consolidations, these movements below
it are always going to be viewed in our eyes as a false breakout. So the false breakout that would trip up
traders otherwise or knock them out of profitable positions if they would have been remaining long, we can
view the marketplace with the market efficiency paradigm, seeing it as a provider of liquidity. See it in just in
the same scope or perspective as a market maker does.

section_title: ALIGNING WITH THE MARKET MAKER'S MOTIVE
content:
And then we're suddenly in line with the market maker. We're not beating the market maker, we're not
trying to outsmart the market maker, all we're trying to do is get in line with what their motive would be.
What's their MO? How are they going to book the market for this particular day or for this month or this
week? How are they going to do that? And by looking at the models that were shown here with looking at
ranges and when they break below the ranges and we see willingness to rally after that, it gives you your
first telltale clue.

section_title: ANTICIPATING ACCUMULATION AND REPRICING
content:
Every time the market goes into consolidation, expect every drop down below an old area of consolidation.
Expect that to be a false breakout and then anticipate accumulation of long positions, and in the market
once they can accumulate their long positions, then they will reprice the market higher running for the buy
stops above old highs.

section_title: MEASURED MOVES FOR PRICE OBJECTIVES
content:
But what else do you see here? If you're looking at this, what else? If you were to study this for a moment
and I'm going to ask you to pause the video, study this for a few minutes and see if there's anything else
that jumps off the chart at you, and don't be discouraged if once I show you what it is if it wasn't apparent.
This first price swing from the first initial false breakout below the old lows and consolidation, once that
rallied up and we gave another area where we can buy again at that 108.75 level, that red area if you will, I
guess it's red, it's a measured move from that same buy and it gives you an approximation over here where
the algorithm will reach for to offer price. And we see it reach up into that 109.25 level from 108.75, which is
the measured move from the initial low that it ran up and created that first red box, and then first that
impulse price swing. The second leg in price higher is equal to that first one.

section_title: LARGER PRICE SWINGS AND PROJECTED RUNS
content:
Also, the larger price swing from that first initial buy all the way up to the intermediate-term high or the
midpoint of the overall price swing, once we had that larger consolidation and it went down and traded into
108.85, when that happened, that same measured move from 108.50 up to 109.45 or thereabouts, that
same measured move from the stop run at 108.85, that gives us a projected run up to that 109.80 level,
and you can see that was handsomely hit.
COALESCENCE:

section_title: LINKING CONCEPTS TO ORDER BLOCKS
content:
And ultimately that 109.90 as we spoke in real time at the time of this recording in the mentorship, which
we spoke about that 109.90 level being directly linked to the bearish order block on the daily chart. And all
these things coalesce, they start to come together, they dovetail beautifully. But understanding each
individual component will help us bridge that vast chasm if you will, of what goes on past the right edge of
your chart.

section_title: PREDICTING PRICE AND LEVELS WITH MARKET MAKER INSIGHT
content:
You see a lot of times where I'll talk about things and I'll give you scenarios of what I think is going to
happen, but specific levels that I think is going to go and be hit. The reason I'm able to do that is because
I'm looking at the market just like I'm showing you here. I'm looking at how the market maker is booking,
how is the market maker manipulating price, what side of the marketplace are they working on, where are
they punishing those that are less informed.

section_title: IDENTIFYING BULLISH OR BEARISH MARKET PROFILES
content:
And if they're seeking one side of the marketplace over and over and over again and they keep expanding
price away from it, in this case it's been the sell stops being run. Every time the sell stops would be run
below a consolidation, it runs higher and it goes through the buy stops. It's just that's the easiest way to see
if we're really in a bullish market or bullish market profile. It's not as easy as everyone says with the
textbooks where you draw a trendline, it's sloping up and just every time it touches the trend line you buy.

section_title: MARKET EFFICIENCY PARADIGM FOR CONSOLIDATIONS
content:
Now you've got to look at where the orders are going to be residing, and again back to that market
efficiency paradigm. By having that model in focus when you look at price, and when you see market
consolidations, when it breaks below that and you start seeing the market trade higher, every time the
market goes in consolidation, they're priming the marketplace to allow sell stops to build up below those
consolidations.

section_title: REVERSED LOGIC FOR BEARISH MARKET PROFILES
content:
And obviously what we've shown here was just for a buy side. If everything was reversed and we were
seeing consolidations in the market rally up, taking the buy stops out and then quickly running lower, and
then moving into a new consolidation and then market running higher, running the buy stops and
accelerating going lower again, everything would just reverse itself in terms of everything we've explained
here.

section_title: CONCLUSION AND UPCOMING MENTORSHIP CONTENT
content:
So I hope this has been insightful to you. I look forward to our third month in the mentorship. So get your
notebooks ready because there's going to be a lot more information by way of price action and we're going
to be focusing in on detailed concepts that have been shown in the free mentorship, free members area on
my website and on the videos, but all the gaps that I left in that free area of teaching, I'm going to fill that in
as we go forward. So with that, guys, I wish you good luck and good trading.
